coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots regulating microstructure of polyamide layer of forward osmosis membrane for anti-biofouling and unexpected dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Biao
Zou, Wensheng
Wu, Pingping
Zhang, Jinhui
Kong, Weili
Chen, Xia
Wu, Chengli
Wang, Yaqin
coredata.dc:description: The introduction of hydrophilic nanomaterials into the polyamide (PA) layer of forward osmosis (FO) membranes usually leads to the enhancement in water flux but a reduction in salt rejection. The decrease in salt rejection, however, inevitably influences the osmotic efficiency and operation cost. In this work, m-phenylenediamine (MPD), one of the chemicals of interfacial polymerization, was utilized as a precursor to synthesize carbon dots (MPD-C-dots) with a good photosensitization functionality. The MPD-C-dots were introduced into the PA layer of FO membranes through co-interfacial polymerization (denoted as Single-C-dots-M), and further grafted onto the PA layer by the conjugation of the residual acyl chloride of trimesoyl chloride with the amine of MPD-C-dots (denoted as Double-C-dots-M). The as-prepared Double-C-dots-M exhibited more outstanding FO performances than the pristine membrane (Control-M) and Single-C-dots-M. Most unexpectedly, the dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection of Double-C-dots-M was found. The further studies reveal that Double-C-dots-M had smaller and more uniform pore size, but larger porosity than the other two. In addition, the biofouling-resistance tests demonstrate that MPD-C-dots-M had an excellent anti-biofouling performance by generating singlet oxygen (1O2) under light irradiation. The proposed Double-C-dots-M provided a typical protocol for improving the performances of FO membrane, especially for the synchronous enhancement in water flux and salt rejection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Forward osmosis (FO)
Carbon dots (C-dots)
Photosensitizer
Biofouling-resistance
Singlet oxygen (1O2)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of control-M, single-C-dots-M, and double-C-dots-M
Evaluation of anti-bacterial property of MPD-C-dots
Fabrication and characterization of control-M, single-C-dots-M, and double-C-dots-M
Evaluation of anti-bacterial performances of TFC-FO membranes
Effects of integration of MPD-C-dots into TFC-FO membranes on FO performance
The possible mechanisms of anti-bacteria and dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection
Possible mechanism for dual-enhancement in water permeability and salt rejection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Research Project of Anhui Province (2022AH050239) and the natural science research key project from Education Department of Anhui Province (KJ2021A0626), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52003003). Authors also thank for the open fund of key laboratory of environment pollution control and waste resource utilization of Anhui Province (K2310003).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and size distribution (B) of MPD-C-dots. FTIR (C), HR O1s (D), C1s (E), and N1s (F) spectra of MPD-C-dots.
Bacterial cultures were treated without (A, C) and with (B, D) MPD-C-dots under irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp using E. coli. (A, B) and S. aureus (C, D) as model strain.

The fluorescence (A), phosphorescence (B), and ATR-FT-IR (C) spectra of Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M. Water contact angles (D) and AFM images (E) of Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M.

Bacteria grown on nutrient plates for colony counting of Control-M (A), Single-C-dots-M (B), and Double-C-dots-M (C) under no light irradiation. Bacteria grown on nutrient plates for colony counting of Control-M (D), Single-C-dots-M (E), and Double-C-dots-M (F) under light irradiation. The normalized water flux (G) of the membranes.
Water flux (Jw) (A), Reverse salt flux (Js) (B), and specific solute flux (Js/Jw) (C) of the Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M.
FTIR analysis of the active layer materials (A), and pore size distribution of active layer (B).
SEM images (up) and the analysis of the average surface porosity of the selected region by ImageJ software (bottom) of the Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M.
The schematic illustration of the synthesis of MPD-C-dots (A), the preparation of Single-C-dots-M and Double-C-dots-M (B), anti-biofouling (C) and FO process of Double-C-dots-M (D).
Intrinsic transport properties of the Control-M, Single-C-dots-M and Double-C-dots-M.
Results of the total porosity of the TFC-FO membranes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Analysis of surface porosity by ImageJ software.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Analysis of total porosity by weighing method.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots regulating microstructure of polyamide layer of forward osmosis membrane for anti-biofouling and unexpected dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Jianzhu University, Anhui, Hefei 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Jianzhu University, Anhui, Hefei 230026, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan, 232001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The introduction of hydrophilic nanomaterials into the polyamide (PA) layer of forward osmosis (FO) membranes usually leads to the enhancement in water flux but a reduction in salt rejection. The decrease in salt rejection, however, inevitably influences the osmotic efficiency and operation cost. In this work, m-phenylenediamine (MPD), one of the chemicals of interfacial polymerization, was utilized as a precursor to synthesize carbon dots (MPD-C-dots) with a good photosensitization functionality. The MPD-C-dots were introduced into the PA layer of FO membranes through co-interfacial polymerization (denoted as Single-C-dots-M), and further grafted onto the PA layer by the conjugation of the residual acyl chloride of trimesoyl chloride with the amine of MPD-C-dots (denoted as Double-C-dots-M). The as-prepared Double-C-dots-M exhibited more outstanding FO performances than the pristine membrane (Control-M) and Single-C-dots-M. Most unexpectedly, the dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection of Double-C-dots-M was found. The further studies reveal that Double-C-dots-M had smaller and more uniform pore size, but larger porosity than the other two. In addition, the biofouling-resistance tests demonstrate that MPD-C-dots-M had an excellent anti-biofouling performance by generating singlet oxygen (1O2) under light irradiation. The proposed Double-C-dots-M provided a typical protocol for improving the performances of FO membrane, especially for the synchronous enhancement in water flux and salt rejection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: To cope with the challenge of global water scarcity, seawater desalination and industrial wastewater treatment are two effective approaches to increase the freshwater supply in many countries and regions. Forward osmosis (FO) driven by the osmotic pressure difference synchronously achieves the cut-through of water and the rejection of salt, exhibiting a tremendous potential in seawater desalination and industrial wastewater treatment [1–5]. Different from pressure-driven membrane processes, FO process features much less energy consumption and much lower membrane fouling than other membrane techniques [6,7]. Thin-film composite (TFC) PA FO membranes have become the research hotspot in the field of FO membranes owing to their outstanding properties such as low operation energy, low membrane fouling, and high water recovery [8,9]. However, the complicated water composition led to the severe challenges toward the long-term operational stability of TFC-PA FO membranes. The resulted membrane fouling, especially for biofouling, greatly contributed to the constant attenuation of membrane flux, continuous reduction of separation efficiency, unceasing deterioration of effluent quality, and further increase of maintenance cost, etc., restricting the applications of TFC-PA membranes in more fields [10,11]. Therefore, many attempts, such as rinsing [12], air-sparging [13,14], osmotic backwashing [15,16], and chemical cleaning [17], etc., have been made to prevent or reduce the detrimental effects of fouling. However, due to the protection provided by extracellular polymer matrix, once biofilm formation occurs, it is difficult to wipe off by physical or chemical treatments [18,19], and other foulants can react with the biofilm to intensify the biofouling [20]. The endowment of TFC-PA FO membranes with inhibiting bacterial proliferation ability by surface modification is the key to resolve this issue [10,21]. Currently, surface coating [22,23], surface functionalization [24,25], and incorporation of tailored materials [26–29], etc., were employed for the biofouling-resistance, successfully realizing the biofouling mitigation and operational lifetime extension of TFC-PA FO membranes. Most regretfully, the modification of anti-biofouling materials usually led to the enhancement in water flux but the reduction in salt interception ratio.

Carbon dots (C-dots), as a new family of quantum dots and a class of zero-dimensional carbon-based fluorescence nanomaterials, have drawn an increasing concern owing to their good biocompatibility, small size, and rich surface functional groups [30,31]. It was reported that C-dots have been integrated at various locations of TFC-PA membranes to regulate the microstructure for the enhancement of membrane properties and realization of the balance between selectivity and permeability. Water channels of TFC-PA membranes can be tuned by introducing hydrophobic C-dots in substrate membrane [32–34]. The coherent finger-like hierarchical channels and microconvex structures in the support layer were promoted to form by the conjugation of C-dots. TFC-FO membrane with 10 wt% load of C-dots exhibited much higher water flux and much lower reverse salt flux than pristine membrane [32]. Amino-rich or polyethyleneimine (PEI) modified C-dots can interfacially polymerize with trimesoyl chloride to form active layer [33–36], greatly bettering the trade-off between permeability and selectivity of TFC-PA FO membrane. Also, amino-rich C-dots, as nanospacers, can enhance the interconnectivity among inner pores. The interlayer established by C-dots has been verified as a progressive orientation for efficient regulation of the membrane structure, which resulted in a thinner and defect-free PA selective layer and optimized the trade-off in permselectivity and the internal concentration polarization (ICP) [37,38]. Chemical grafting of C-dots on the PA layer has improved the chlorine resistance performance, as well as permeability [39]. Additionally, C-dots are identified as the next-generation artificial enzymes that can mimic the catalytic center of natural enzymes [40]. The integration of C-dots onto TFC-PA membranes has made them achieve excellent anti-bacterial properties, which was beneficial to prolong the operational lifetime of TFC-PA membranes [32–39,41,42]. However, similar to the tailored nanomaterials mentioned above, the trade-off in various aspects after the integration of C-dots occurred, but the works on the dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection were barely reported [43]. These two properties seem like a pair of incompatible contradictions.

Inspired by the strategies of using C-dots to regulate the microstructure of TFC-PA membranes for improving their FO properties, in this work, C-dots were synthesized by a solvothermal approach using MPD as a carbon resource (denoted as MPD-C-dots), on which the amines were expected to be the same meta-position structure as MPD molecules. The MPD-C-dots were introduced into the PA layer through co-interfacial polymerization (denoted as Single-C-dots-M), and further modified onto the PA layer by the conjugation of residual acyl chloride with the amine of C-dots (denoted as Double-C-dots-M). The surface wettability, fluorescence spectra, and roughness of the as-prepared Double-C-dots-M were evaluated by the contact angle, fluorescence spectrophotometer, and atomic force microscope (AFM), respectively. The FO performances of the three pieces of membranes, including the pristine membrane (Control-M), were measured and compared. Additionally, the mechanisms of biofouling resistance, and the dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection of Double-CDs-M were detailedly elucidated by BET pore size analysis and ImageJ software.

The m-phenylenediamine (MPD, purity 99%) was the product of Macklin (Shanghai, China). 1,3,5-Benzenetri‑carbonyl trichloride (TMC, purity over 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC8739) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC6538) were purchased from Shanghai Lu Wei Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP, purity 99%) and N, N-Dimethylacetamide (DMAC, purity 99%) were obtained from Tianjin BODI Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All others were analytical reagent grade and purchased commercially and used as received.

MPD (0.10 g) was dissolved in 15 mL ethanol under stirring, and this solution was then transferred into 20 mL of autoclaves. After heating at 180 °C in an oven for 720 min and cooling down to room temperature, a gray suspension was obtained. The large particles in suspension were removed by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The same volume of ultrapure (UP) water was added into the supernatant and then concentrated by a rotary evaporation at 60 °C. The MPD-C-dots powder was obtained by freeze-drying and stored in a refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of control-M, single-C-dots-M, and double-C-dots-M
ce:section.ce:para: PES substrate membrane was prepared according to our previous reports [41,42]. In brief, PES support layer was cleaned up by UP water prior to the interfacial polymerization (IP), and then dried in air. Hereafter, the PES membrane was covered with 0.2 wt% MPD aqueous solution containing 0.1 wt% MPD-C-dots for 3 min. After removal of the excess MPD aqueous solution by compressed air, hexane solution containing 0.15 wt% TMC was poured onto the membrane surface for reacting 2.4 min. The unreacted TMC/hexane solution was cleared away from the membrane surface, then dried at 60 °C for 8 min to completely eliminate the excessive hexane (Single-C-dots-M). After the membrane cooled, 0.1 wt% MPD-C-dots aqueous solution was uniformly dispersed on the surface of the membrane for 30 min (Double-C-dots-M). The clean membranes were reserved in UP water for subsequent experiments.

The performance in FO mode were evaluated using a FO set-up at room temperature, where the effective surface area of a membrane was 9.62 cm2. UP water was set as feed solution. The solutions were pushed by peristaltic pumps at the velocity of flow of 0.5 L/min. The weight change of water permeation with time was recorded by an electronic scale. The water flux ( Jw , unit: Lm−2 h−1 (LMH)) and reverse salt flux ( Js , unit: gm−2 h−1 (gMH)) were obtained by the following Eqs. (1) (2), respectively:
(1)
Jw =
∆ V
membrane surface area × Δt
(2)
Js =
∆
Ct ∙ Vt
membrane surface area × Δt
where ΔV is the permeation volume change over the test time Δt, Ct and Vt are the salt concentration and volume of feed solution, respectively. Cp and Cf are the salt concentrations in the permeation and feed solution, respectively.

The intrinsic permeability and resistance to sodium chloride of membranes were measured in RO mode, more details were presented in Supplementary Information (SI) (4. Calculation of intrinsic transport parameter.)

MPD molecules are usually utilized to establish the compact skin layer of PA membranes through the IP. Use of MPD as a carbon source can guarantee the amine groups on the C-dots to maintain the meta-position structure as much as possible, which is helpful to improve the uniformity of the PA layer. Therefore, MPD-C-dots were fabricated using MPD as a precursor by heating MPD ethanol solution at 180 °C in an oven for 12 h (Scheme 1A). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image, zeta-potential measurement, and X-Ray diffraction (XRD) pattern were conducted to characterize the as-prepared C-dots (1. Apparatus and characterization in SI). TEM image displays that the MPD-C-dots were all well-dispersed, and a well-spherical morphology was found (Fig. 1A). The size of the as-prepared C-dots distributed from 1.6 to 5.4 nm in diameter, and their average size was 3.2 nm (Fig. 1B). The Zeta-potential was measured to be 16.5 mV, revealing the positively charged nature of MPD-C-dots, which may be caused by amines covered on the MPD-C-dots (Fig. S1). Besides, the structural feature of MPD-C-dots was further examined through an XRD analysis. The XRD patterns of MPD-C-dots show a broad peak at ∼24.1° and a shoulder peak at ∼43.7°, indicating that the MPD-C-dots are an amorphous structure (Fig. S2A) [44]. These two diffraction peaks corresponded to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of carbon, respectively [45].

FT-IR spectrum can predict the reaction activity and design the post-modification path by identifying the functional groups of the nanomaterials. The formation and chemical structure of MPD-C-dots were analyzed by comparing the difference of FT-IR spectra of MPD-C-dots and MPD molecules. As shown in Fig. 1A, the new characteristic peaks at 1378 cm−1, 1261 cm−1, and 1223 cm−1 in the FT-IR curve of MPD-C-dots were observed, which are assigned to the CN, CO, and aromatic C-NH2 stretching vibrations, respectively [46–48]. A weak characteristic peak of –COOH at 1700 cm−1 was found in the FT-IR curve of MPD-C-dots, which can be attributed to be the oxidation of water in solution [49]. In addition, the difference ranging from 3200 to 3400 cm−1 demonstrated the occurrence of chemical reaction among MPD molecules during carbonization at high temperature.

Moreover, XPS spectra were carried out to further confirm the FT-IR results. The C, O, and N elements with corresponding binding energies at 530.6, 284.4, and 399.2 eV, respectively, were found in the XPS survey spectrum of MPD-C-dots (Fig. S2B). The high-resolution (HR) O1s XPS curve has peaks at 531.4 and 533.0 eV, which belongs to CO and CO bonds, respectively (Fig.1D). MPD is an oxygen-free molecule. We speculated that the O element may originate from the water in ethanol, and water as an oxidant participated the redox reaction during carbonization [50]. The HR C1s XPS spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 284.9, 286.4 and 287.7 eV (Fig. 1E), which can be attributed to C-C/C=C, CN, and CO groups, respectively [48]. Three peaks at 401.1, 400.0 and 398.9 eV displayed in the HR N1s spectrum are related to pyrrolic N, amino N, and pyridinic N, respectively (Fig. 1F) [44]. These results confirm that the successful synthesis of MPD-C-dots, and the surface of MPD-C-dots was covered with a small amount of carboxylic acid groups but a large amount of amines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of anti-bacterial property of MPD-C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent photophysicochemical performances of C-dots are the foundation as nanozymes and photodynamic anti-bacterial chemotherapy reagents [51,52]. Prior to the anti-bacterial tests, we investigated the photophysicochemical characters of MPD-C-dots (Optical properties of MPD-C-dots were addressed in SI). MPD-C-dots can synchronously emit fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) under the excitation of 365 nm UV lamp. The fluorescence and RTP are tri-exponential nature, and the lifetime can be fitted to be 2.05 ns and 0.4 μs, respectively. Most interestingly, the RTP emission implied that the triplet exciton can transfer to triplet oxygen (3O2) to generate singlet oxygen (1O2) by an energy transfer pathway [41,42]. Therefore, MPD-C-dots were considered as a photosensitizer for photodynamic anti-bacteria here. E. coli and S. aureus were co-cultured with and without MPD-C-dots (16 mg/L). The amount of bacterial colonies in the plate with MPD-C-dots under irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp was far less than that under light irradiation only (Fig. 2 ). The anti-bacterial efficiencies of MPD-C-dots were calculated to be 98.3% for E. coli and 99.1% for S. aureus via quantitative assay, revealing that the MPD-C-dots have a huge potential as a photodynamic anti-bacterial reagent, and laying a good foundation to be integrated MPD-C-dots into PA membrane for the highly efficient anti-biofouling property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of control-M, single-C-dots-M, and double-C-dots-M
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of biofilm inevitably causes an adverse effect on the separation efficacy of the membrane, resulting in a short operation lifetime and high maintenance cost. Many strategies have been adopted to better their anti-bacterial properties and minimize the formation of biofilm [53]. In consideration of the excellent anti-bacterial potential exhibited by our MPD-C-dots, MPD-C-dots were expected to integrate into TFC-PA membranes and achieve the desirable anti-biofouling performance. As mentioned above, MPD as a precursor to synthesize C-dots can make amines at surface retain the meta-position structure. Naturally, MPD-C-dots were utilized as co-IP reagent to participate the first IP reaction and establish a novel active layer on the surface of the PES substrate membrane (2. Preparation of TFC-FO membrane in SI), which was denoted as Single-C-dots-M. Meanwhile, at the same conditions but without MPD-C-dots, the pristine membrane was also fabricated (Control-M). After IP reaction, a portion of residual acyl chloride can sequentially take part in the second IP reaction to endow TFC-PA membranes with desirable characteristics (Scheme 1 B) [54]. Therefore, the MPD-C-dots were conjugated to the skin layer of Single-C-dots-M through the reaction between residual acyl chloride and amine of MPD-C-dots. The resulted TFC-PA membrane was denoted as Double-C-dots-M.

The characterizations such as optical performances, contact angle, zeta-potential, and AFM of the Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M were carried out. The trimesic acid molecule itself can emit fluorescence and RTP due to the existence of aromatic ring and aromatic carbonyl [49]. After IP reaction, the formation of amido bond can boost the RTP emission through a charge transfer pathway, namely the lone-electron pair of –NH– transfers toward the oxygen of aromatic carbonyl due to the electron-withdrawing effect of carbonyl [41]. The fluorescence and RTP emissions of the above-mentioned TFC-PA membranes were studied. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence of Control-M was weak. The stronger fluorescence of Single-C-dots-M, the strongest fluorescence of Double-C-dots-M were found due to the introduction of MPD-C-dots through twice IP reaction.

The RTP emission behaviors of Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M gave the same result as the fluorescence based on the similar reason. The difference lied in that the RTP of Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M gave rise to redshift because of the change of chemical microenvironment (Fig. 3B). ATR-FTIR was performed to analyze the functional group changes of Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the peaks at 1544 and 1657 cm−1 are corresponded to NH bending and CN stretching vibration in the amide bond, and CO stretching vibration [55], respectively. The peak at 1607 cm−1 is the NH deformed stretching [56]. The signal enhancements of CO, N-H/C-N and NH bands, together with the fluorescence and RTP emission behaviors, demonstrate the successful integration of MPD-C-dots into the PA layer and onto the membranes.

The surface wettability of the above-mentioned PA membranes was characterized by water contact angle [57,58]. The results of 76.35 ± 2.75° for Control-M, 58.9 ± 1.2° for Single-C-dots-M, and 43.8 ± 1.5° for Double-C-dots-M were obtained (Fig. 3D). We found that the water contact angles were constantly decreased with the PMD-C-dots being coupled into the active layer and constantly being integrated onto the surface. Double-C-dots-M showed a best wettability due to a great number of the hydrophilic amino groups from the MPD-C-dots anchored on the surface. Moreover, the surface zeta-potential of membranes, −21.48 mV for Control-M, 10.23 mV for Single-C-dots-M, and 21.31 mV for Double-C-dots-M, were determined (Fig. S6). Also, AFM images were conducted to learn about the smoothness of membranes. The results show that there are many protrusions on the surface of membranes. The smoothness gradually increased from Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, to Double-C-dots-M in turn (Fig. 3E). The surface roughness was calculated. The Ra = 60.7 nm for Control-M, Ra = 57.8 nm for Single-C-dots-M, and Ra = 41.4 nm for Double-C-dots-M were obtained, which is roughly consistent with the AFM images. Significantly, the surface roughness of Double-C-dots-M was effectively decreased after integrating MPD-C-dots into TFC-PA membrane via twice IP reaction. The SEM cross-section of the Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M gave the same result (Fig. S5). The improvement in surface roughness can minimize the biofilm formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of anti-bacterial performances of TFC-FO membranes

ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the anti-bacterial activities of the different membranes were evaluated using E. coli and S. aureus as model bacteria (3.1. Static anti-bacterial in SI) (Scheme 1C). No light irradiation, three membranes grew bacterial colonies on agar plates in different degrees. The bacterial colonies of E. coli and S. aureus are Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M in the descending order due to cation anti-bacterial effect (Fig. 4A-C, A'-C'). Under irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, the widespread E. coli and S. aureus on the nutrient plate of Control-M were observed (Fig. 4D, D'), indicating the very poor or no anti-bacterial activity of Control-M. Compared with Control-M, the amount of E. coli and S. aureus on the nutrient plate of Single-C-dots-M seemed very sparse and far less than that of Control-M (Fig. 4E, E'), implying a boosted anti-bacterial activity for Single-C-dots-M (∼44%). Most significantly, Double-C-dots-M exhibited the strongest anti-bacterial activity (near to ∼100%), and almost no bacteria was found to grow on the nutrient plate (Fig. 4F, F'). These results manifest that the integration of MPD-C-dots into TFC-PA FO membranes endowed the membranes with outstanding biofouling-resistance except for the contribution of amines of MPD-C-dots. The dramatic increase of anti-bacterial activity on the Single-C-dots-M and Double-C-dots-M was rooted in the outstanding photosensitization property of MPD-C-dots, which can generate 1O2, a famous photodynamic chemotherapy anti-bacterial reagent, under light irradiation (vide post).

To simulate the biofouling degree during membrane operation, the dynamic anti-bacterial activities of the three different membranes were tested (3.2. Dynamic anti-bacterial in SI). The effects of the accelerated biofouling on the water flux of the three pieces of membranes were presented in Fig. 4G. Both Control-M and Single-C-dots-M showed a downward trend after stable maintenance for a period of time (∼300 min), but the decreasing extent of Single-C-dots-M was much smaller. Whereas the water flux of the Double-C-dots-M remained unchanged for a much longer time (∼500 min) before decay. Obviously, the adhesion and growth of E. coli resulted in the obstruction of membrane pores, contributing on these declines [59]. These results coincide with the static anti-bacterial tests (Fig. 4A-C and Fig. 4A’-C′), contact angles measurement (Fig. 3D), and AFM image results (Fig. 3E), in which the Double-C-dots-M displayed the highest anti-bacterial activities and the lowest surface roughness, resulting in the significantly improving the anti-biofouling performance and greatly decreasing the interfacial adhesion. The durability of the membranes was considered here. Firstly, the dynamic anti-bacterial results reveal that even in the case of accelerated biofouling, the membranes can maintain stable operation for a period of time (Fig. 4G). Secondly, the effect of the operation time on water flux of the Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, and Double-C-dots-M has been assessed. The result shows that water flux of the modified membranes remained relatively stable over 100 h of runtime at 4 bar of operational pressure (Fig. S7). These results indicate that the modified membranes owned favourable mechanical (shear) strength. Similarly, the modified membranes exhibited more excellent anti-fouling properties than Control-M using an organic small molecule as a model (Anti-fouling Performance of the membranes in SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of integration of MPD-C-dots into TFC-FO membranes on FO performance
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the decreases of surface hydrophobicity and roughness will improve the permeability of the PA membranes [22–29,60]. FO performances of three pieces of membranes were investigated and compared here. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, the water fluxes gradually increased from Control-M, Single-C-dots-M to Double-C-dots-M. These results are in accord with the betterment both in wettability and smoothness after the conjugation of MPD-C-dots into TFC-PA membrane. In a word, the hydrophilicity enhancement and roughness reduction of FO membranes based on MPD-C-dots modification greatly ameliorated the water flux. Usually, the continuous operation of FO process in a high salt environment inevitably made FO membranes apparently swell, which intensified the inner concentration polarity (ICP) and weakened the FO performance [61]. The effect of the amount of MPD-C-dots on the osmotic performance was simply surveyed (Fig. S9). As the increase of concentration of MPD-C-dots to 0.1 wt%, the water flux of Single-C-dots-M and Double-C-dots-M gradually increased to a peak. Further increase of MPD-C-dots content led to the decrease of water flux, which can attribute to the high load of MPD-C-dots increased the thickness of the selective layer, resulting in the pore clogging and aggregation of MPD-C-dots [43,62].

When DI water and 2 M NaCl were used as a feed solution and a draw solution, respectively, the Js of 6.166 gMH for Control-M, 4.987 gMH for Single-C-dots-M, and 3.839 gMH for Double-C-dots-M were obtained (Fig. 5B). The Js gradually decreased with the increasing load of MPD-C-dots, which mainly resulted from C-dots regulating the pore size distribution of TFC-PA FO membranes to be smaller and more uniform (vide post), weakening the adverse effect of membrane swelling. Also, the intrinsic transport properties of different PA membranes were assessed (4. Calculation of intrinsic transport parameters in SI). As listed in Table 1 , the parameters (A, B), where A and B represent the water and salt permeability, respectively, (1.26, 0.311) for Control-M, (1.42, 0.291) for Single-C-dots-M, and (1.57, 0.232) for Double-C-dots-M were achieved. From Control-M, Single-C-dots-M, to Double-C-dots-M, the increasing A values and decreasing B values demonstrated that the modification of MPD-C-dots indeed enhanced the FO performances of TFC-PA membranes. The comprehensive parameter (B/A), (0.247) for Control-M, (0.205) for Single-C-dots-M, and (0.148) for Double-C-dots-M, were worked out, giving the similarity to B value. The advantage using B/A lies in that the FO trend was expressed with one parameter, and in reality includes double meaning.

Most unexpectedly, the salt rejection of 90.4% for Control-M, 92.6% for Single-C-dots-M, and 95.3% for Double-C-dots-M were detected under RO mode (Scheme 1D). The result of specific solute flux (Js/Jw) presented in Fig. 5C indicates that the membrane exhibited much better salt rejection performance with the integration of the PMD-C-dots. These results can be attributed to the smaller and more uniform pore size tuned by MPD-C-dots. Moreover, the apparent structural parameter (S), 227.93 for Control-M, 166.64 for Single-C-dots-M, and 122.16 for Double-C-dots-M, were worked out, suggesting that the integration of the MPD-C-dots onto TFC-FO membrane are in favor of decreasing S and weakening ICP.

The conjugation of hydrophilic nanomaterials into the PA membranes usually results in the improvement in water flux but reduction in salt rejection due to the improvement in both hydrophilicity and smoothness. Reversely, because of the same meta-structure of amines of MPD-C-dots as MPD molecule, the smaller and more uniform pore size, and larger porosity of PA layer were tuned out by twice IP reactions (vide post), realizing the enhancement both in water flux and salt rejection (Table S3), and starting up a promising direction for the accurate regulation of microstructure of active layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanisms of anti-bacteria and dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection

ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the highly efficient anti-biofouling performances of TFC-PA membranes modified by MPD-C-dots, and MPD-C-dots themselves, it's of great necessity to comprehend the anti-bacterial essence, which is conductive to design and tailor biofouling-resistance nanomaterials. MPD-C-dots can emit fluorescence at 460 nm and RTP at 527 nm (Fig. S3C). The triplet excitons of RTP are inclined to transfer their energy to dissolved oxygen through an energy transfer approach, resulting in the generation of 1O2 [41,42]. The TMB oxidation method was performed to study the essence of MPD-C-dots as a photosensitizer. When MPD-C-dots were added into TMB aqueous solution for 1 min under light irradiation, the blue solution was observed, indicating the formation of oxTMB. To testify the 1O2 as an oxidant of TMB oxidation, tryptophan, 1O2 scavenger, was dosed into the system containing MPD-C-dots and TMB at the same conditions. The unchanged system colour proved the photosensitization function of MPD-C-dots (Fig. S10A). Besides, the appearance of characteristic peak of 1O2 with three equal intensity signals in the EPR spectra also gave the same result (Fig. S10B) [41,42]. A mass of amines anchored on the surface of MPD-C-dots also contributed to the biofouling-resistance, which further limited the biofilm formation, significantly improving the separation performance of FO membranes [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for dual-enhancement in water permeability and salt rejection
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth exploring the mechanism of abnormal dual-enhancement in water permeability and salt rejection. The same peaks as those in Fig. 3C were observed in the ART-FT-IR spectra of active layer powders of these modified membranes (Fig. 6A). It was reported that when the minimum pore size of the membrane is regulated down to ∼1.3 nm, these sub-nanopores just like water channels can promote the water penetration, while preventing the hydrated ions and other pollutants from permeating [64]. We studied the pore size distributions of three pieces of membranes by BET. As shown in Fig. 6B, the pore size distributions mainly within 2.1–6.5 nm for Control-M, 2.0–5.4 nm for Single-C-dots-M, and 1.8–2.1 nm for Double-C-dots-M were found. It can be seen that with the integration of MPD-C-dots, the pore size distribution gradually narrowed down to the little-end. Especially, the pore size distribution of Double-C-dots-M is rather narrow and uniform, which contributed to the enhanced salt rejection [64]. These can be attributed to the addition of MPD-C-dots, which reacts with the acyl chloride to boost the degree of cross-linking of PA layer, decreasing the pore size [62]. Moreover, MPD-C-dots preferentially filled the macropore on the surface during SIP, further narrowing the pore size. Therefore, the membrane pore size has been reduced to an unexpectedly critical size by introducing Double-C-dots-M, which can promote water penetration but prevent the hydrated ions and other pollutants from permeating.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) coupling with ImageJ software has been used for quantifying the porosity of the immersion-precipitation membrane [65]. The SEM images of these modified membranes were obtained at 10000-fold magnification (Fig. 7 -up). As observed, all membranes are an asymmetric structure with a dense skin layer on a porous spongy support. ImageJ software was then performed for the SEM image analysis to determine the average surface porosity of the selected region (Fig.7-bottom). For each sample, the SEM surface image is taken from a random location [66]. Surface porosity ε (%) was calculated by Eq. (3) as below:
(3)
ε =
Sp Sm
× 100 %
where Sm (nm2) and Sp (nm2) are the total area of pores in SEM image and the area of support surface in the same SEM image, respectively.

The results listed in Table 2 show the surface porosities of 44.19% for Control-M, 53.95% for Single-C-dots-M, and 63.79% for Double-C-dots-M, indicating that the integration of hydrophilic nanomaterials, such as MPD-C-dots, into membrane can alter the surface structure, and improve the characteristics and performance of membrane [67]. In addition, the weighing method was also employed to survey the total porosities of membranes (5. Total porosity analysis by weighing method in SI) [68]. The total porosities of 61.1% for Control-M, 67.7% for Single-C-dots-M, and 76.7% for Double-C-dots-M were obtained (Table 2), which is in agreement with that of ImageJ. Therefore, the dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection were satisfactorily resolved by the smaller and more uniform pore size, but the larger porosity of the active layer was tuned by the twice integrations of MPD-C-dots.

In summary, MPD-C-dots with meta-structural amines on the surface were synthesized by a solvothermal approach using MPD as a precursor, which can synchronously emit fluorescence and RTP. Single-C-dots-M and Double-C-dots-M were developed through IP reaction and successive twice IP reaction, respectively. MPD-C-dots were secondary integrated into PA active layer, endowing PA membranes with an excellent anti-bacterial performance. The betterment in both hydrophilicity and smoothness of TFC-PA membranes led to the increase in water flux, along with the abnormal enhancement in salt rejection. SEM image together with ImageJ software assay reveals that the twice integrations of MPD-C-dots precisely regulated the microstructure of PA layer. As a result, the smaller and more uniform pore size, but larger porosity of PA layer were realized. Accordingly, the mechanism of dual-enhancement in water flux and salt rejection was successfully elucidated.

Biao Zhang: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Wensheng Zou: Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Pingping Wu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Jinhui Zhang: Formal analysis. Weili Kong: Formal analysis. Xia Chen: Methodology. Chengli Wu: Methodology. Yaqin Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Research Project of Anhui Province (2022AH050239) and the natural science research key project from Education Department of Anhui Province (KJ2021A0626), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52003003). Authors also thank for the open fund of key laboratory of environment pollution control and waste resource utilization of Anhui Province (K2310003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.susmat.2024.e00910.
